panel.origin: Two opposite prefixes: 'hyper-' means excessive or above normal; 'hypo-' means deficient or below normal. They attach to countless terms to flag too much or too little of something.
panel.mixology: The patient was hypertensive on arrival (high blood pressure) but became hypotensive after the bleed, her pressure dropping dangerously low.
panel.examples: From Greek 'hyper' (over, above) and 'hypo' (under, beneath). The same roots give us everyday words like 'hyperactive' and 'hypothermia'.

panel.origin: A suffix meaning inflammation. Attach it to a body part and you have the condition of that part being inflamed — one of the most common building blocks in all of medicine.
panel.mixology: Appendicitis, tonsillitis, arthritis, bronchitis — once you know '-itis' means inflammation, half the chart starts to decode itself.
panel.examples: From Greek, originally an adjective ending meaning 'pertaining to'. It came to specifically denote inflammatory disease in medical Latin.

panel.origin: A suffix meaning surgical removal of something. Attach it to an organ or structure and it names the operation to take that thing out.
panel.mixology: She underwent an appendectomy (removal of the appendix); a hysterectomy removes the uterus, a mastectomy the breast.
panel.examples: From Greek 'ektomē' (a cutting out), from 'ek' (out) + 'temnein' (to cut). The same 'temnein' root runs through much of surgical vocabulary.

panel.origin: '-otomy' means cutting into (an incision); '-ostomy' means creating a permanent opening. One cuts in; the other makes a lasting mouth or passage.
panel.mixology: A tracheotomy is an incision into the windpipe; a colostomy creates a permanent opening from the colon to the abdominal wall.
panel.examples: Both from Greek: '-otomy' from 'temnein' (to cut), '-ostomy' from 'stoma' (mouth, opening). 'Stoma' is still used alone for the opening itself.

panel.origin: A suffix meaning the study or science of something. It names both fields of medicine and the specialists who practice them (via '-ologist').
panel.mixology: Cardiology is the study of the heart, so a cardiologist treats it; neurology, the nervous system; oncology, cancer.
panel.examples: From Greek 'logos' (word, study, reason). It's one of the most productive suffixes in all of English, far beyond medicine.

panel.origin: A suffix referring to a condition of the blood. It signals that the root word describes something about the blood's state or contents.
panel.mixology: Anemia is a deficiency of healthy red blood cells; hypoglycemia is low blood sugar; leukemia is a cancer of the blood-forming tissues.
panel.examples: From Greek 'haima' (blood) — the same root behind 'hemoglobin' and 'hemorrhage'. Spelled '-aemia' in British usage.

panel.origin: A suffix meaning pain. Attach it to a body part and it names pain in that location.
panel.mixology: Neuralgia is nerve pain; myalgia, muscle pain; arthralgia, joint pain. Fibromyalgia combines fiber, muscle, and pain.
panel.examples: From Greek 'algos' (pain). The same root gives 'analgesic' — literally a substance that takes pain away.

panel.origin: Two opposite prefixes for speed: 'brady-' means slow; 'tachy-' means fast. Most often applied to heart rate and breathing.
panel.mixology: Bradycardia is a slow heart rate; tachycardia, a fast one. A monitor alarm for 'tachy' means the heart is racing.
panel.examples: From Greek 'bradys' (slow) and 'tachys' (swift) — the latter also behind 'tachometer', the gauge measuring engine speed.

panel.origin: Directional terms: 'anterior' means toward the front of the body; 'posterior' means toward the back. They orient where a structure or injury is.
panel.mixology: The anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) sits at the front of the knee; a posterior approach enters from the back.
panel.examples: From Latin 'ante' (before) and 'post' (after, behind). 'Ventral' (belly) and 'dorsal' (back) are near-synonyms, especially in anatomy.

panel.origin: Directional terms relative to the body's center: 'proximal' means closer to the trunk or point of origin; 'distal' means farther away.
panel.mixology: The wrist is distal to the elbow; the shoulder is proximal. A distal fracture of the radius is near the wrist end of the bone.
panel.examples: From Latin 'proximus' (nearest) and 'distare' (to stand apart) — the same roots behind 'proximity' and 'distant'.

panel.origin: Body positions: 'supine' is lying face-up on the back; 'prone' is lying face-down. Critical for exams, surgery, and positioning patients.
panel.mixology: Place the patient supine for the abdominal exam; prone positioning is used to improve oxygenation in some respiratory failure.
panel.examples: From Latin 'supinus' (lying on the back) and 'pronus' (bent forward). A memory aid: 'supine' contains 'up' — face up.

panel.origin: Directional terms: 'lateral' means toward the side, away from the midline; 'medial' means toward the midline of the body.
panel.mixology: The little toe is on the lateral side of the foot; the big toe is medial. A lateral ankle sprain affects the outer ligaments.
panel.examples: From Latin 'latus' (side) and 'medius' (middle). 'Bilateral' (both sides) and 'median' share these roots.

panel.origin: A combining form meaning heart. It opens a huge family of terms about the heart and circulation.
panel.mixology: Cardiology, cardiac arrest, cardiomyopathy, electrocardiogram (ECG/EKG) — all built on the heart root.
panel.examples: From Greek 'kardia' (heart). The same root underlies 'cardiac' and, more distantly, the idea of the heart as the body's core.

panel.origin: A combining form meaning skin. It anchors the vocabulary of skin health and disease.
panel.mixology: Dermatology is the study of skin; dermatitis is skin inflammation; the dermis is the layer beneath the surface epidermis.
panel.examples: From Greek 'derma' (skin). 'Epidermis' literally means 'upon the skin' — the outermost layer.

panel.origin: A combining form meaning stomach, and by extension the digestive tract.
panel.mixology: Gastritis is stomach inflammation; gastroenteritis adds the intestines; a gastroenterologist treats the whole digestive system.
panel.examples: From Greek 'gastēr' (stomach, belly). The same root appears in 'gastronomy' — the art and science of good eating.

panel.origin: Two ways to describe a condition's timeline: 'acute' means sudden onset and short duration; 'chronic' means long-lasting or persistent.
panel.mixology: An acute asthma attack comes on fast and resolves; chronic asthma is an ongoing condition managed for years.
panel.examples: From Latin 'acutus' (sharp, pointed) and Greek 'chronos' (time). 'Chronic' literally means 'of time' — defined by its duration.

panel.origin: Swelling caused by excess fluid trapped in the body's tissues. A common, visible sign with many possible causes.
panel.mixology: The patient had pitting edema in both ankles — pressing a finger left a dent — pointing the team toward a heart or kidney workup.
panel.examples: From Greek 'oidēma' (a swelling). Spelled 'oedema' in British usage. The related 'edematous' describes the swollen tissue.

panel.origin: The medical term for fainting — a temporary loss of consciousness from a brief drop in blood flow to the brain.
panel.mixology: She experienced syncope after standing too quickly; the episode was brief, and she recovered fully within a minute.
panel.examples: From Greek 'synkopē' (a cutting short) — also, fittingly, a music term for an interrupted rhythm. Pronounced 'SIN-ko-pee'.

panel.origin: Difficult or labored breathing; the sensation of shortness of breath. One of the most important symptoms to assess.
panel.mixology: The patient reported dyspnea on exertion — breathless after climbing one flight of stairs — prompting a cardiac and pulmonary evaluation.
panel.examples: From Greek 'dys-' (difficult, bad) + 'pnoia' (breathing). The 'dys-' prefix recurs everywhere: dysfunction, dysphagia, dysuria.

panel.origin: 'Febrile' means having a fever; 'afebrile' means without fever. Basic but constant vocabulary in any clinical setting.
panel.mixology: The child arrived febrile at 103°F; by morning she was afebrile and alert, and the team relaxed.
panel.examples: From Latin 'febris' (fever) — the same root behind 'fever' itself. The 'a-' prefix in 'afebrile' means 'without'.

panel.origin: A state of drowsiness, sluggishness, and reduced alertness. A subjective but clinically meaningful sign of how sick someone is.
panel.mixology: The normally energetic toddler was lethargic and hard to rouse, a red flag that escalated the urgency of the visit.
panel.examples: From Greek 'lēthargia' (drowsiness), linked to 'Lethe', the mythological river of forgetfulness in the underworld.

panel.origin: Describes a disease or condition with no known cause. A precise, honest label for 'we don't know why this is happening'.
panel.mixology: After every test came back normal, the diagnosis was idiopathic — the cause of her condition simply couldn't be identified.
panel.examples: From Greek 'idios' (one's own, peculiar) + 'pathos' (suffering, disease). One wit defined it as 'idiot doctors, pathetic patients' — a joke, not the etymology.

panel.origin: Affecting both sides of the body. Its opposite, 'unilateral', means one side only.
panel.mixology: Bilateral pneumonia means both lungs are involved; a unilateral weakness raises concern for a stroke on one side of the brain.
panel.examples: From Latin 'bi-' (two) + 'latus' (side). The same 'latus' root behind 'lateral' and 'medial'.

panel.origin: Removing a small sample of tissue to examine it, usually to diagnose or rule out disease such as cancer.
panel.mixology: The suspicious mole was removed and sent for biopsy; the pathology report confirmed it was benign.
panel.examples: From Greek 'bios' (life) + 'opsis' (sight) — literally 'viewing of living tissue'. A 'necropsy' (autopsy) views the dead instead.

panel.origin: Complete Blood Count — one of the most common lab tests, measuring red cells, white cells, and platelets to give a broad snapshot of health.
panel.mixology: Her CBC showed a high white-cell count, supporting the suspicion of an infection driving the fever.
panel.examples: A modern clinical abbreviation. Each component (hemoglobin, hematocrit, WBC) carries its own roots, but 'CBC' itself is plain initials.

panel.origin: Two imaging tests: MRI (Magnetic Resonance Imaging) uses magnets and radio waves for detailed soft-tissue pictures; CT (Computed Tomography) uses X-rays for fast cross-sectional views.
panel.mixology: The CT scan was ordered first to rule out a bleed quickly; an MRI followed for a finer look at the soft tissue.
panel.examples: MRI is an initialism for its physics; CT comes from Greek 'tomos' (a slice, a cut) + 'graphein' (to write) — imaging by slices.

panel.origin: Inserting a tube into the airway (the trachea) to keep it open and allow mechanical breathing support.
panel.mixology: As the patient's breathing failed, the team moved to intubation, securing the airway before connecting the ventilator.
panel.examples: From Latin 'in' (into) + 'tuba' (tube). The reverse procedure, removing the tube, is 'extubation'.

panel.origin: A thin, flexible tube inserted into the body to drain fluid, deliver medication, or access a vessel. The term covers many types.
panel.mixology: A urinary catheter was placed to monitor output; a central catheter gave direct access to a large vein for medications.
panel.examples: From Greek 'kathetēr' (something let down or inserted), from 'kathienai' (to send down). One of the oldest words in surgical practice.

panel.origin: An order meaning 'nothing by mouth' — no food or drink, usually before surgery or certain tests to keep the stomach empty.
panel.mixology: The patient was kept NPO after midnight in preparation for the morning operation.
panel.examples: From Latin 'nil per os' (nothing through the mouth). One of many Latin abbreviations that survive in modern medication and nursing orders.

panel.origin: Intravenous — into or within a vein. Refers to the line, the access, and the route for delivering fluids and medications directly into the bloodstream.
panel.mixology: They started an IV to deliver fluids and antibiotics; 'IV push' means giving a medication directly and quickly through that line.
panel.examples: From Latin 'intra' (within) + 'vena' (vein). The contrast 'IM' is intramuscular — into the muscle instead.

panel.origin: An order meaning immediately, without delay. One of the most urgent words in a hospital.
panel.mixology: 'Get me a STAT chest X-ray' means now — the order jumps to the front of the line.
panel.examples: From Latin 'statim' (immediately, at once). One of the most recognizable bits of medical shorthand, even outside the field.

panel.origin: An instruction meaning 'as needed' — a medication or action to be done when required rather than on a fixed schedule.
panel.mixology: Pain medication was ordered PRN, so the patient received it only when the pain returned, not by the clock.
panel.examples: From Latin 'pro re nata' (literally 'for the thing born', i.e. as the situation arises). A staple of prescription writing.

panel.origin: Dosing frequencies: 'BID' means twice a day; 'TID' means three times a day. 'QID' (four times) and 'QD' (once daily) round out the family.
panel.mixology: The antibiotic was prescribed BID — one tablet morning and evening — for ten days.
panel.examples: From Latin 'bis in die' (twice a day) and 'ter in die' (three times a day). These Latin dosing codes remain standard on prescriptions.

panel.origin: Common medical shorthand: 'Rx' means prescription or treatment; 'Dx' means diagnosis. 'Sx' (symptoms) and 'Hx' (history) complete the set.
panel.mixology: The chart listed the Dx as pneumonia and the Rx as a course of antibiotics plus rest.
panel.examples: 'Rx' is often traced to the Latin 'recipe' (take thou), or to the symbol of the Roman god Jupiter; 'Dx', 'Sx', 'Hx' use 'x' as a general abbreviation marker.

panel.origin: Short for vital signs — the core measurements of body function: temperature, pulse, respiratory rate, blood pressure, and often oxygen saturation.
panel.mixology: 'Vitals are stable' is shorthand for reassurance; a sudden change in vitals is often the first warning of trouble.
panel.examples: From Latin 'vitalis' (of life), from 'vita' (life). They're called 'vital' because they signal whether the body's basic life functions are holding.

panel.origin: 'Hx' means history — most often the patient's medical background. 'PMH' is past medical history; 'FHx' is family history.
panel.mixology: Her Hx included asthma and a prior surgery; the PMH guided which medications were safe to give.
panel.examples: 'Hx' uses 'H' for history plus 'x' as the standard abbreviation marker, matching Dx, Sx, and Rx in clinical shorthand.
